sections.title: Use
rows: To convey what someone wanted to know
'**Did** you **finish** the report?' she asked him. → She asked him **if** he **had finished** the report.

sections.title: Useful notes
items: In reported questions, **whether** can be used as an alternative to **if**.
She asked him whether he had finished the report.

sections.title: Be careful
items: Do not use question word order in reported questions.

items.correct: She asked where I had parked.

items.incorrect: She asked where had I parked.

exercises.title: A: Reported yes/no questions
exercises.instructions: Rewrite the direct questions as reported questions using if.
exercises.studentInstruction: Rewrite the direct questions as reported questions using if.

items.displayPrompt: "Are you feeling better?" the nurse asked me.

cues: "Are you feeling better?" the nurse asked me.

items.answer: The nurse asked me if I was feeling better.
acceptedAnswers: The nurse asked me if I was feeling better.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Yes/no question, so the sentence is "The nurse asked me if I was feeling better.".
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: Prompt: "Are you feeling better?" the nurse asked me. → The nurse asked me if I was feeling better.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Passive voice — be + past participle when the action matters more than who did it.

wrongOptions.Yesno: Check how "Yesno" changes in the target sentence — tense or word form may shift.
wrongOptions.question: Check how "question" changes in the target sentence — tense or word form may shift.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Match the tense shift shown in the explanation — one verb often moves back one step in time.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Write one complete sentence with correct punctuation — capital letter at the start and a full stop at the end.

items.displayPrompt: "Have you sent the email?" Liam asked.

cues: "Have you sent the email?" Liam asked.

items.answer: Liam asked if I had sent the email.
acceptedAnswers: Liam asked if I had sent the email.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Present perfect, so the sentence is "Liam asked if I had sent the email.".
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: Prompt: "Have you sent the email?" Liam asked. → Liam asked if I had sent the email.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect links past actions to now — has/have + past participle; yet goes at the end of negatives.

wrongOptions.Present: Check how "Present" changes in the target sentence — tense or word form may shift.
wrongOptions.perfect: Check how "perfect" changes in the target sentence — tense or word form may shift.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Match the tense shift shown in the explanation — one verb often moves back one step in time.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Write one complete sentence with correct punctuation — capital letter at the start and a full stop at the end.

items.displayPrompt: "Can you speak Italian?" Sara asked Tom.

cues: "Can you speak Italian?" Sara asked Tom.

items.answer: Sara asked Tom if he could speak Italian.
acceptedAnswers: Sara asked Tom if he could speak Italian.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Can, so the sentence is "Sara asked Tom if he could speak Italian.".
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: Prompt: "Can you speak Italian?" Sara asked Tom. → Sara asked Tom if he could speak Italian.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Reported speech backshifts tenses and time/place words (now → then, tomorrow → the next day, here → there).

wrongOptions.Can: Check how "Can" changes in the target sentence — tense or word form may shift.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Match the tense shift shown in the explanation — one verb often moves back one step in time.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Write one complete sentence with correct punctuation — capital letter at the start and a full stop at the end.

items.answer: Mum asked if they would arrive that night.
acceptedAnswers: Mum asked if they would arrive that night.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Tonight, so the sentence is "Mum asked if they would arrive that night.".
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: Prompt: "Will they arrive tonight?" Mum asked. → Mum asked if they would arrive that night.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Reported speech backshifts tenses and time/place words (now → then, tomorrow → the next day, here → there).

wrongOptions.Tonight: Check how "Tonight" changes in the target sentence — tense or word form may shift.

explanationContent.commonMistake: In reported speech, change time and place words (tomorrow → the next day, here → there).
explanationContent.usefulTip: Write one complete sentence with correct punctuation — capital letter at the start and a full stop at the end.

items.answer: Daniel asked if they should wait outside.
acceptedAnswers: Daniel asked if they should wait outside.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Should we, so the sentence is "Daniel asked if they should wait outside.".
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: Prompt: "Should we wait outside?" Daniel asked. → Daniel asked if they should wait outside.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Build a grammatically complete sentence that matches the prompt: Daniel asked if they should wait outside.

wrongOptions.Should: Check how "Should" changes in the target sentence — tense or word form may shift.
wrongOptions.we: Check how "we" changes in the target sentence — tense or word form may shift.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Match the tense shift shown in the explanation — one verb often moves back one step in time.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Write one complete sentence with correct punctuation — capital letter at the start and a full stop at the end.

items.displayPrompt: "Did you lock the door?" Dad asked me.

cues: "Did you lock the door?" Dad asked me.

items.answer: Dad asked me if I had locked the door.
acceptedAnswers: Dad asked me if I had locked the door.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Past question, so the sentence is "Dad asked me if I had locked the door.".
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: Prompt: "Did you lock the door?" Dad asked me. → Dad asked me if I had locked the door.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Second conditional: If + past simple, would + base verb — unreal present/future situations.

wrongOptions.Past: Check how "Past" changes in the target sentence — tense or word form may shift.
wrongOptions.question: Check how "question" changes in the target sentence — tense or word form may shift.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Match the tense shift shown in the explanation — one verb often moves back one step in time.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Write one complete sentence with correct punctuation — capital letter at the start and a full stop at the end.

exercises.title: B: Reported wh- questions
exercises.instructions: Complete each reported question with the correct form.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete each reported question with the correct form.

items.sentence: "Where does your cousin work?" he asked. → He asked where my cousin ...... .
acceptedAnswers: worked

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is wh- question, so the correct answer is "worked".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Compare your answer with the grammar or vocabulary clue in the sentence — tense, form, or collocation may be wrong.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Relative clauses add detail about a noun — choose the pronoun that matches the antecedent.

items.sentence: "Why are they laughing?" she asked. → She asked why they ...... laughing.
acceptedAnswers: were

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is present continuous, so the correct answer is "were".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: "When will the concert finish?" I asked. → I asked when the concert ...... .
acceptedAnswers: would finish

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is will, so the correct answer is "would finish".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "would finish", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: "Who did you invite?" Emma asked. → Emma asked who I ...... .
acceptedAnswers: had invited

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is past question, so the correct answer is "had invited".

wrongOptions.invited: Without had, the earlier-past relationship is lost — use past perfect for the first of two past actions.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "had invited", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Relative clauses add detail about a noun — choose the pronoun that matches the antecedent.

items.sentence: "How long did the film last?" Tom asked. → Tom asked how long the film ...... .
acceptedAnswers: had lasted

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is past, so the correct answer is "had lasted".

wrongOptions.lasted: Without had, the earlier-past relationship is lost — use past perfect for the first of two past actions.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Past simple only shows one past event — use past perfect when one past action happened before another.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Past perfect describes an action that happened before another past action.

items.sentence: "Which colour do you prefer?" Mia asked. → Mia asked which colour I ...... .
acceptedAnswers: preferred

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is present, so the correct answer is "preferred".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Compare your answer with the grammar or vocabulary clue in the sentence — tense, form, or collocation may be wrong.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Relative clauses add detail about a noun — choose the pronoun that matches the antecedent.

items.displayPrompt: "Open your books," the teacher said to us.

cues: "Open your books," the teacher said to us.

items.answer: The teacher told us to open our books.
acceptedAnswers: The teacher told us to open our books.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Order, so the sentence is "The teacher told us to open our books.".
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: Prompt: "Open your books," the teacher said to us. → The teacher told us to open our books.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Build a grammatically complete sentence that matches the prompt: The teacher told us to open our books.

wrongOptions.Order: Check how "Order" changes in the target sentence — tense or word form may shift.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Match the tense shift shown in the explanation — one verb often moves back one step in time.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Write one complete sentence with correct punctuation — capital letter at the start and a full stop at the end.

items.displayPrompt: "Don't park here," the police officer said to me.

cues: "Don't park here," the police officer said to me.

items.answer: The police officer told me not to park there.
acceptedAnswers: The police officer told me not to park there.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Negative order, so the sentence is "The police officer told me not to park there.".
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: Prompt: "Don't park here," the police officer said to me. → The police officer told me not to park there.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Reported speech backshifts tenses and time/place words (now → then, tomorrow → the next day, here → there).

wrongOptions.Negative: Check how "Negative" changes in the target sentence — tense or word form may shift.
wrongOptions.order: Check how "order" changes in the target sentence — tense or word form may shift.

explanationContent.commonMistake: In reported speech, change time and place words (tomorrow → the next day, here → there).
explanationContent.usefulTip: Write one complete sentence with correct punctuation — capital letter at the start and a full stop at the end.

items.displayPrompt: "Please carry this bag," Grandma said to Leo.

cues: "Please carry this bag," Grandma said to Leo.

items.answer: Grandma asked Leo to carry that bag.
acceptedAnswers: Grandma asked Leo to carry that bag.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Request, so the sentence is "Grandma asked Leo to carry that bag.".
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: Prompt: "Please carry this bag," Grandma said to Leo. → Grandma asked Leo to carry that bag.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Relative clauses add detail — choose who/which/that/whose to match the noun they describe.

wrongOptions.Request: Check how "Request" changes in the target sentence — tense or word form may shift.

explanationContent.commonMistake: In reported speech, change time and place words (tomorrow → the next day, here → there).
explanationContent.usefulTip: Write one complete sentence with correct punctuation — capital letter at the start and a full stop at the end.

items.displayPrompt: "Please don't make any noise," the librarian said to the children.

cues: "Please don't make any noise," the librarian said to the children.

items.answer: The librarian asked the children not to make any noise.
acceptedAnswers: The librarian asked the children not to make any noise.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Polite request, so the sentence is "The librarian asked the children not to make any noise.".
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: Prompt: "Please don't make any noise," the librarian said to the children. → The librarian asked the children not to make any noise.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Passive voice — be + past participle when the action matters more than who did it.

wrongOptions.Polite: Check how "Polite" changes in the target sentence — tense or word form may shift.
wrongOptions.request: Check how "request" changes in the target sentence — tense or word form may shift.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Match the tense shift shown in the explanation — one verb often moves back one step in time.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Write one complete sentence with correct punctuation — capital letter at the start and a full stop at the end.

items.sentenceBefore: "Do you live nearby?" he asked me. He asked me

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The context points to "if I lived" — yes/no.

wrongOptions.B: "if did I live" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "if I lived" is correct.
wrongOptions.if did I live: "if did I live" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "if I lived" is correct.
wrongOptions.C: "whether I live" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "if I lived" is correct.
wrongOptions.whether I live: "whether I live" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "if I lived" is correct.
wrongOptions.D: "that I lived" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "if I lived" is correct.
wrongOptions.that I lived: "that I lived" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "if I lived" is correct.

explanationContent.usefulTip: Eliminate options that break grammar first, then check which remaining word fits the meaning.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "if I lived" is the only option that matches both meaning and register in this sentence.

items.sentenceBefore: "Where did you put the keys?" she asked. She asked

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is wh-, so the correct answer is "where I had put".

wrongOptions.A: "where did I put" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "where I had put" is correct.
wrongOptions.where did I put: "where did I put" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "where I had put" is correct.
wrongOptions.C: "where had I put" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "where I had put" is correct.
wrongOptions.where had I put: "where had I put" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "where I had put" is correct.
wrongOptions.D: "where I putted" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "where I had put" is correct.
wrongOptions.where I putted: "where I putted" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "where I had put" is correct.

explanationContent.usefulTip: Eliminate options that break grammar first, then check which remaining word fits the meaning.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "where I had put" is the only option that matches both meaning and register in this sentence.

items.sentenceBefore: "Please wait here," the receptionist said. The receptionist

wrongOptions.A: "asked me wait" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "told me to wait" is correct.
wrongOptions.asked me wait: "asked me wait" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "told me to wait" is correct.

wrongOptions.D: "asked that I waited" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "told me to wait" is correct.
wrongOptions.asked that I waited: "asked that I waited" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "told me to wait" is correct.

explanationContent.usefulTip: Eliminate options that break grammar first, then check which remaining word fits the meaning.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "told me to wait" is the only option that matches both meaning and register in this sentence.

items.sentenceBefore: "Don't touch the screen," Dad said. Dad told us

wrongOptions.A: "to not touch" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "not to touch" is correct.
wrongOptions.to not touch: "to not touch" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "not to touch" is correct.

wrongOptions.not touch: "not touch" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "not to touch" is correct.

explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "not to touch" is the only option that matches both meaning and register in this sentence.

wrongOptions.A: "can" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "could" is correct.
wrongOptions.can: "can" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "could" is correct.

items.sentenceBefore: "When will the shop close?" he asked. He asked when the shop

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The context points to "would close" — will.

wrongOptions.A: "will close" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "would close" is correct.
wrongOptions.will close: "will close" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "would close" is correct.
wrongOptions.C: "did close" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "would close" is correct.

explanationContent.grammarFocus: Future forms (will, going to, present continuous) depend on whether the sentence predicts, plans, or arranges.

exercises.title: E: Key word transformation
exercises.instructions: Complete the second sentence so that it has a similar meaning.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete the second sentence so that it has a similar meaning.

items.sentence:
"Do you play tennis?" she asked me. **IF**
She asked me ...... tennis.
acceptedAnswers: if I played

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is yes/no, so the correct answer is "if I played".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "if I played", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "if I played" is a phrasal verb or fixed chunk that collocates with the rest of the sentence.

items.sentence:
"Where did you find that wallet?" Tom asked. **WHERE**
Tom asked ...... that wallet.
acceptedAnswers: where I had found

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is wh-, so the correct answer is "where I had found".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "where I had found", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Relative clauses add detail about a noun — choose the pronoun that matches the antecedent.

items.sentence:
"Please text me later," Amy said to Ben. **ASKED**
Amy ...... later.
acceptedAnswers: asked Ben to text her

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is request, so the correct answer is "asked Ben to text her".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "asked Ben to text her", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence:
"Don't shout in class," the teacher said. **TOLD**
The teacher ...... in class.
acceptedAnswers: told us not to shout

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is order, so the correct answer is "told us not to shout".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "told us not to shout", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence:
"When are you moving house?" I asked. **WHEN**
I asked ...... house.
acceptedAnswers: when they were moving

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is wh-, so the correct answer is "when they were moving".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "when they were moving", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence:
"Can I use your charger?" Lucy asked me. **WHETHER**
Lucy asked me ...... my charger.
acceptedAnswers: whether she could use

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is permission, so the correct answer is "whether she could use".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "whether she could use", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.
